question: What is the difference between sequential-access storage and direct-access storage?
mock_answer: Sequential-access storage, such as magnetic tape, requires data to be accessed from the beginning of the medium in sequence. In contrast, direct-access storage, which includes magnetic disks and SSDs, allows data to be read from any location on the storage device without needing to process preceding data.
rubric: Must identify tape storage as an example of sequential-access storage.
Must state that sequential access requires reading from the beginning of the medium.
Must identify magnetic disk and/or SSD storage as examples of direct-access storage.
Must state that direct access allows reading data from any location which is faster than tape access as that requires reading from the start of the tape.
gold_chunks: tape storage is referred to as sequential-access storage.
In contrast, magnetic disk and SSD storage are referred to as direct-access storage because it is possible to read data from any location on disk.
However, access to data is much slower because the tape must be accessed sequentially from the beginning of the tape; tapes can be very long, requiring tens to hundreds of seconds to access data.

rubric: Must identify tape storage as an example of sequential-access storage.
Must state that sequential access requires reading from the beginning of the medium.
Must identify magnetic disk and/or SSD storage as examples of direct-access storage.
Must state that direct access allows reading data from any location which is faster than tape access as that requires reading from the start of the tape.

question: What are the primary functions of a disk controller?
mock_answer: A disk controller serves as the interface between the computer system and the disk drive's hardware. Its functions include accepting high-level commands to read or write a sector, attaching checksums to data for error detection, retrying reads if an error is detected, and remapping bad sectors to spare physical locations on the disk.
rubric: Must state that the disk controller is an interface between the computer system and the disk hardware.
Must mention that it accepts high-level read or write commands for sectors.
May list or mention some its other functions: attaching checksums, retrying failed reads, or remapping bad sectors.
gold_chunks: A disk controller interfaces between the computer system and the actual hardware of the disk drive; in modern disk systems, the disk controller is implemented within the disk drive unit.
A disk controller accepts high-level commands to read or write a sector, and initiates actions, such as moving the disk arm to the right track and actually reading or writing the data.
Disk controllers also attach checksums to each sector that is written; the checksum is computed from the data written to the sector.

When the sector is read back, the controller computes the checksum again from the retrieved data and compares it with the stored checksum; if the data are corrupted, with a high probability the newly computed checksum will not match the stored checksum.
If such an error occurs, the controller will retry the read several times; if the error continues to occur, the controller will signal a read failure.
Another interesting task that disk controllers perform is remapping of bad sectors.

If the controller detects that a sector is damaged when the disk is initially formatted, or when an attempt is made to write the sector, it can logically map the sector to a different physical location (allocated from a pool of extra sectors set aside for this purpose).

composites: Disk controllers also attach checksums to each sector that is written; the checksum is computed from the data written to the sector.

When the sector is read back, the controller computes the checksum again from the retrieved data and compares it with the stored checksum; if the data are corrupted, with a high probability the newly computed checksum will not match the stored checksum.
If such an error occurs, the controller will retry the read several times; if the error continues to occur, the controller will signal a read failure.
Another interesting task that disk controllers perform is remapping of bad sectors.

If the controller detects that a sector is damaged when the disk is initially formatted, or when an attempt is made to write the sector, it can logically map the sector to a different physical location (allocated from a pool of extra sectors set aside for this purpose).

rubric: Must state that the disk controller is an interface between the computer system and the disk hardware.
Must mention that it accepts high-level read or write commands for sectors.
May list or mention some its other functions: attaching checksums, retrying failed reads, or remapping bad sectors.

composites: Disk controllers also attach checksums to each sector that is written; the checksum is computed from the data written to the sector.

If the controller detects that a sector is damaged when the disk is initially formatted, or when an attempt is made to write the sector, it can logically map the sector to a different physical location (allocated from a pool of extra sectors set aside for this purpose).

question: In the context of RAID, what is mirroring?
mock_answer: Mirroring, also known as shadowing, is a redundancy technique where every disk is duplicated. In a mirrored system, a logical disk is composed of two physical disks, and every write operation is performed on both. This allows data to be read from the second disk if the first one fails, thereby improving reliability.
rubric: Must define mirroring as the duplication of data across multiple disks.
Must state that writes are carried out on both or all disks in the mirrored pair or sets.
Must explain that its purpose is redundancy, allowing data to be read from the other disk in case of a failure.
gold_chunks: The simplest (but most expensive) approach to introducing redundancy is to duplicate every disk.
This technique is called mirroring (or, sometimes, shadowing).
A logical disk then consists of two physical disks, and every write is carried out on both disks.
If one of the disks fails, the data can be read from the other.

composites: The simplest (but most expensive) approach to introducing redundancy is to duplicate every disk.
This technique is called mirroring (or, sometimes, shadowing).
A logical disk then consists of two physical disks, and every write is carried out on both disks.
If one of the disks fails, the data can be read from the other.

rubric: Must define mirroring as the duplication of data across multiple disks.
Must state that writes are carried out on both or all disks in the mirrored pair or sets.
Must explain that its purpose is redundancy, allowing data to be read from the other disk in case of a failure.

composites: The simplest (but most expensive) approach to introducing redundancy is to duplicate every disk.
This technique is called mirroring (or, sometimes, shadowing).
A logical disk then consists of two physical disks, and every write is carried out on both disks.
If one of the disks fails, the data can be read from the other.

question: How does a flash memory system handle page updates?
mock_answer: Since a page in flash memory cannot be directly overwritten, the system uses a flash translation layer to manage updates. This layer maps logical page numbers to physical page numbers. When a logical page is updated, the new data is written to a different, pre-erased physical page, and the mapping in the translation table is updated. The original physical page is marked as deleted and is later erased as part of a larger erase block, which contains multiple pages. This out-of-place update mechanism avoids the slow process of erasing a single page before writing to it.
rubric: Must state that a flash page cannot be directly overwritten and must be erased first.
Must explain that the erase operation must be  performed on a group of pages that is called an erase block.
May describe how flash memory systems counter the slowness of erase blocks by mapping logical page numbers to physical page numbers.
gold_chunks: However, once written, a page of flash memory cannot be directly overwritten.
Instead, it has to be erased and rewritten subsequently.
The erase operation must be performed on a group of pages, called an erase block, erasing all the pages in the block, and takes about 2 to 5 milliseconds.
Flash memory systems limit the impact of both the slow erase speed and the update limits by mapping logical page numbers to physical page numbers.

All the above actions are carried out by a layer of software called the flash translation layer; above this layer, flash storage looks identical to magnetic disk storage, providing the same page/sector-oriented interface, except that flash storage is much faster.

composites: However, once written, a page of flash memory cannot be directly overwritten.
Instead, it has to be erased and rewritten subsequently.

question: Compare and contrast bit-level striping and block-level striping in RAID systems.
mock_answer: Both bit-level and block-level striping are RAID techniques that improve performance by striping data across multiple disks. Bit-level striping splits the bits of each byte across the disks, meaning every disk must participate in every access. This increases the data-transfer rate for a single large access but does not increase the number of concurrent accesses. In contrast, block-level striping distributes entire blocks across the disks. This allows for parallel reads of multiple blocks for large files and also permits different disks to service separate single-block requests simultaneously, increasing overall I/O throughput. Due to its superior flexibility and performance for mixed workloads, block-level striping is used in practical systems, whereas bit-level striping is not.
rubric: Must identify that both are striping techniques for performance improvement.
Must correctly describe bit-level striping as splitting the bits of each byte across disks.
Must correctly describe block-level striping as distributing entire blocks across disks.

Must compare their performance impacts: bit-level striping improves the transfer rate for a single access, while block-level striping improves both large-access transfer rates and the throughput of small, independent accesses.
May state that block-level striping is used in practice while bit-level is not.
gold_chunks: With multiple disks, we can improve the transfer rate as well (or instead) by striping data across multiple disks.
In its simplest form, data striping consists of splitting the bits of each byte across multiple disks; such striping is called bit-level striping.

For example, if we have an array of eight disks, we write bit i of each byte to disk i. In such an organization, every disk participates in every access (read or write), so the number of accesses that can be processed per second is about the same as on a single disk, but each access can read eight times as much data in the same time as on a single disk.
Block-level striping stripes blocks across multiple disks.
When reading a large file, block-level striping fetches n blocks at a time in parallel from the n disks, giving a high data-transfer rate for large reads.
When a single block is read, the datatransfer rate is the same as on one disk, but the remaining n -1 disks are free to perform other actions.
Block-level striping offers several advantages over bitlevel striping, including the ability to support a larger number of block reads per second, and lower latency for single block reads.
As a result, bit-level striping is not used in any practical system.

rubric: Must identify that both are striping techniques for performance improvement.
Must correctly describe bit-level striping as splitting the bits of each byte across disks.
Must correctly describe block-level striping as distributing entire blocks across disks.

Must compare their performance impacts: bit-level striping improves the transfer rate for a single access, while block-level striping improves both large-access transfer rates and the throughput of small, independent accesses.
May state that block-level striping is used in practice while bit-level is not.
gold_chunks: With multiple disks, we can improve the transfer rate as well (or instead) by striping data across multiple disks.
In its simplest form, data striping consists of splitting the bits of each byte across multiple disks; such striping is called bit-level striping.

For example, if we have an array of eight disks, we write bit i of each byte to disk i. Block-level striping offers several advantages over bitlevel striping, including the ability to support a larger number of block reads per second, and lower latency for single block reads.
As a result, bit-level striping is not used in any practical system.

question: Explain the components of disk access time and how they contribute to the total time required to read or write data from a magnetic disk.
mock_answer: The access time for a magnetic disk is the time elapsed from when a read or write request is issued to when the data transfer begins. It is composed of two main components: seek time and rotational latency. Seek time is the mechanical delay caused by moving the disk arm to position the read-write head over the correct track. Rotational latency is the time spent waiting for the disk platter to rotate so that the desired sector is positioned directly under the head. The total access time is the sum of the seek time and the rotational latency, and it represents the overhead incurred before any data can actually be transferred.
rubric: Must define access time as the time from request issuance to the start of data transfer.
Must identify seek time as a component of access time and define it as the time to move the disk arm.
Must state that the total access time is the sum of seek time and rotational latency.
Must identify rotational latency as a component of access time and define it as the time waiting for the sector to rotate into position.
gold_chunks: Access time is the time from when a read or write request is issued to when data transfer begins.

To access (i.e., to read or write) data on a given sector of a disk, the arm first must move so that it is positioned over the correct track, and then must wait for the sector to appear under it as the disk rotates.
The time for repositioning the arm is called the seek time, and it increases with the distance that the arm must move.
Once the head has reached the desired track, the time spent waiting for the sector to be accessed to appear under the head is called the rotational latency time.
The access time is then the sum of the seek time and the latency; average access times range from 5 to 20 milliseconds depending on the disk model.
Each disk platter has a flat, circular shape. Its two surfaces are covered with a magnetic material, and information is recorded on the surfaces.

composites: The time for repositioning the arm is called the seek time, and it increases with the distance that the arm must move.
Once the head has reached the desired track, the time spent waiting for the sector to be accessed to appear under the head is called the rotational latency time.
The access time is then the sum of the seek time and the latency; average access times range from 5 to 20 milliseconds depending on the disk model.

rubric: Must define access time as the time from request issuance to the start of data transfer.
Must identify seek time as a component of access time and define it as the time to move the disk arm.
Must state that the total access time is the sum of seek time and rotational latency.
Must identify rotational latency as a component of access time and define it as the time waiting for the sector to rotate into position.
gold_chunks: Access time is the time from when a read or write request is issued to when data transfer begins.

To access (i.e., to read or write) data on a given sector of a disk, the arm first must move so that it is positioned over the correct track, and then must wait for the sector to appear under it as the disk rotates.
The time for repositioning the arm is called the seek time, and it increases with the distance that the arm must move.
Once the head has reached the desired track, the time spent waiting for the sector to be accessed to appear under the head is called the rotational latency time.

question: A database administrator is choosing between an SSD and a magnetic disk for a new server. The server's primary workload consists of a high volume of small, random data lookups. Based on their physical mechanisms and performance measures, which storage type is more suitable and why?
mock_answer: An SSD is the more suitable choice for a workload with a high volume of small, random data lookups. The key reason lies in the difference in access time. A magnetic disk's performance on random accesses is poor because each lookup requires a mechanical seek operation to move the disk arm and a rotational latency delay for the platter to spin into position, resulting in access times of 5 to 10 milliseconds. In contrast, an SSD has no moving parts and can access any page of data electronically, with a latency of only 20 to 100 microseconds. This fundamental difference in physical operation means an SSD can service a much higher number of random I/O operations per second (IOPS), making it far superior for this type of workload.
rubric: Must recommend the SSD as the more suitable choice.
Must identify that the critical performance metric for this workload is random access time/latency.
Must explain that a magnetic disk's random access time is high due to mechanical factors, specifically seek time and rotational latency.
Must explain that an SSD's random access time is extremely low because it is electronic.
Must connect the lower latency of SSDs to their ability to support a much higher number of I/O operations per second (IOPS).
Must use the comparative latency values (milliseconds for disks vs. microseconds for SSDs) to justify the conclusion.
Must explain that an SSD's random access time is extremely low because it has no moving parts.
Must explain that an SSD's random access time is extremely low because it can parallelize requests.
gold_chunks: To access (i.e., to read or write) data on a given sector of a disk, the arm first must move so that it is positioned over the correct track, and then must wait for the sector to appear under it as the disk rotates.
The time for repositioning the arm is called the seek time, and it increases with the distance that the arm must move.
The time spent waiting for the sector to be accessed to appear under the head is called the rotational latency time.
In contrast, in a random access pattern, successive requests are for blocks that are randomly located on disk.
Each such request would require a seek.

Compared to magnetic disks, SSDs can provide much faster random access: the latency to retrieve a page of data ranges from 20 to 100 microseconds for SSDs, whereas a random access on disk would take 5 to 10 milliseconds.

The number of I/O operations per second ( IOPS ), that is, the number random block accesses that can be satisfied by a disk in a second, depends on the access time, and the block size, and the data transfer rate of the disk.

A solid-state drive ( SSD ) uses flash memory internally to store data but provides an interface similar to a magnetic disk, allowing data to be stored or retrieved in units of a block; such an interface is called a block-oriented interface.
Solid-state disks (SSDs) are built using NAND flash and provide the same block-oriented interface as disk storage.

Unlike magnetic disks, SSDs can support multiple random requests in parallel, with 32 parallel requests being commonly supported; a flash disk with SATA interface supports nearly 100,000 random 4-kilobyte block reads in a second with 32 requests sent in parallel, while SSDs connected using NVMe PCIe can support over 350,000 random 4-kilobyte block reads per second.

composites: In contrast, in a random access pattern, successive requests are for blocks that are randomly located on disk.
Each such request would require a seek.

rubric: Must recommend the SSD as the more suitable choice.
Must identify that the critical performance metric for this workload is random access time/latency.
Must explain that a magnetic disk's random access time is high due to mechanical factors, specifically seek time and rotational latency.
Must explain that an SSD's random access time is extremely low because it is electronic.
Must connect the lower latency of SSDs to their ability to support a much higher number of I/O operations per second (IOPS).
Must use the comparative latency values (milliseconds for disks vs. microseconds for SSDs) to justify the conclusion.
Must explain that an SSD's random access time is extremely low because it has no moving parts.
Must explain that an SSD's random access time is extremely low because it can parallelize requests.

The time for repositioning the arm is called the seek time, and it increases with the distance that the arm must move.

A solid-state drive ( SSD ) uses flash memory internally to store data but provides an interface similar to a magnetic disk, allowing data to be stored or retrieved in units of a block; such an interface is called a block-oriented interface.
Solid-state disks (SSDs) are built using NAND flash and provide the same block-oriented interface as disk storage.

Unlike magnetic disks, SSDs can support multiple random requests in parallel, with 32 parallel requests being commonly supported; a flash disk with SATA interface supports nearly 100,000 random 4-kilobyte block reads in a second with 32 requests sent in parallel, while SSDs connected using NVMe PCIe can support over 350,000 random 4-kilobyte block reads per second.

Each such request would require a seek.

question: A system uses RAID with mirroring, where a single disk has a mean time to failure (MTTF) of 50,000 hours and a mean time to repair of 4 hours. What is the mean time to data loss of a mirrored disk system for this mirrored system? Why is the mean time to data loss for the mirrored system significantly higher than the MTTF of a single disk, and what underlying assumption is critical for this high reliability?
mock_answer: The mean time to data loss in a mirrored system is 312,500,000 (or 312.4 * 10^6) seconds. This is vastly higher than a single disk's MTTF because data loss occurs only if the second disk fails during the brief window when the first failed disk is being repaired. Since every write is duplicated, the failure of one disk does not cause data loss; the system continues to operate using the mirrored copy. The probability of the second disk also failing within the short 10-hour mean time to repair is extremely low. The critical assumption for calculating this high level of reliability is that the failures of the two disks are independent events. If a single event, like a power failure or natural disaster, could cause both disks to fail simultaneously, this assumption would be violated and the actual reliability would be much lower than the calculated value.
rubric: Must explain that data loss only occurs if the second disk fails while the first is being repaired.
Must contrast the short repair window (mean time to repair) with the long operational life of a disk (MTTF) to explain why the probability of data loss is very low.
Must identify the critical assumption as the independence of disk failures.
May provide an example of a non-independent failure event, such as a power failure or natural disaster.

Must mention the mirrored disk system's mean time to failure is 312,500,000 (or 312.4 * 10^6) seconds. The value can be written in any other format as long it is approximately 300 million seconds then it is correct.
gold_chunks: This technique is called mirroring (or, sometimes, shadowing).
If one of the disks fails, the data can be read from the other.
Data will be lost only if the second disk fails before the first failed disk is repaired.

The mean time to failure (where failure is the loss of data) of a mirrored disk depends on the mean time to failure of the individual disks, as well as on the mean time to repair, which is the time it takes (on an average) to replace a failed disk and to restore the data on it.
Suppose that the failures of the two disks are independent; that is, there is no connection between the failure of one disk and the failure of the other.

Then, if the mean time to failure of a single disk is 100,000 hours, and the mean time to repair is 10 hours, the mean time to data loss of a mirrored disk system is 100,000 2 /(2 * 10) = 500 * 10 6 hours, or 57,000 years!
You should be aware that the assumption of independence of disk failures is not valid.
Power failures and natural disasters such as earthquakes, fires, and floods may result in damage to both disks at the same time.

composites: This technique is called mirroring (or, sometimes, shadowing).
If one of the disks fails, the data can be read from the other.
Data will be lost only if the second disk fails before the first failed disk is repaired.
Suppose that the failures of the two disks are independent; that is, there is no connection between the failure of one disk and the failure of the other.

Then, if the mean time to failure of a single disk is 100,000 hours, and the mean time to repair is 10 hours, the mean time to data loss of a mirrored disk system is 100,000 2 /(2 * 10) = 500 * 10 6 hours, or 57,000 years!
You should be aware that the assumption of independence of disk failures is not valid.
